items.evidence_text:
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill down. Michae, what’s the household gross income these days—ballpark for the year?
Client 1: All-in, it’s about two twenty-five a year. Roughly.
Advisor: Okay, about $225k gross household. Margaret, does that sound right to you?
Client 2: Yeah—around that. I always think of it as like two-twenty-something.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing things up—Michae you’re Client 1, Margaret you’re Client 2, right?

Client 1: Total out the door is about thirteen thousand a month.
Client 2: Yeah, total. I was thinking net of the mortgage at first—sorry.
Advisor: No, that’s super common. So total monthly expenses about $13,000. Then let’s isolate the debt piece inside that. What are the monthly debt payments—mortgage plus any other loan—roughly?
Client 2: Mortgage is… three thousand? Three-one?
Client 1: It’s $3,150 a month on the mortgage.

Client 1: It’s $3,150 a month on the mortgage.
Client 2: And the other one is like two hundred a month.
Advisor: Okay—$3,150 plus $200, so about $3,350 per month in debt payments. That sound right?
Client 1: Yes, $3,350 total debt payments monthly.
Advisor: Great. While we’re on it, the mortgage—what’s the balance and rate, roughly?

Advisor: Thanks. Switching to assets—when you think investable assets, what’s the total across brokerage, retirement accounts, cash—roughly?
Client 1: Around $1.2 million. Maybe a hair over.
Advisor: Okay, about $1.22 million investable. Let’s break that down so I’m not guessing. You’ve got a joint taxable brokerage—what’s that sitting at?
Client 1: About $765,000.
Client 2: That’s the big one, yeah.

Client 1: Yeah, tiny. About $10,150 at the bank.
Advisor: Okay. And then separate from investable assets, your home—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client 2: Around $314,000.
Client 1: $314,100 is what we’ve been using.
Advisor: Perfect. Now income—Michae, you mentioned business income. How does that come in—weekly, monthly?

Client 1: It’s not a card. It’s a loan, but yeah, same idea, high rate.
Advisor: Okay, thanks for correcting me—installment loan with a high rate, we’ll treat it as non-mortgage debt either way.
Advisor: Now, if I add those two balances together—mortgage plus that loan—that should be your total outstanding loans. Does that total come to about $92,600?
Client 2: Wait, I thought we owed like ninety-five?
Client 1: No—$89,500 plus $3,050 is $92,550, so yeah, call it $92,600 total outstanding.

Client 1: Yes, $3,350 total debt payments monthly.
Advisor: Great. While we’re on it, the mortgage—what’s the balance and rate, roughly?
Client 1: Balance is about $89,500, rate is 4.8%.
Client 2: And it ends in… 2028? I think October?
Advisor: October 2028—perfect. And that other loan—what is it, like a credit card or an actual loan?

Client 1: It’s a loan, not a card. High rate though.
Advisor: Got it—loan. What’s the balance and interest rate on that one?
Client 1: About $3,050 outstanding, and it’s ugly—23.18%.
Client 2: I thought it was twenty-one percent.
Client 1: No, I checked the statement last month—23.18%.

Advisor: Alright—thanks, Michae. Going back to that gross-versus-net thing Margaret brought up earlier, I want to make sure I’m not mixing terms on the planning side either.
Client 2: Yeah, I get lost when it gets too… spreadsheet-y.
Advisor: Totally fair. So big-picture, when markets wobble, how would you describe your comfort level—are you more conservative, moderate, aggressive?
Client 1: Conservative. I’m not trying to swing for the fences.
Client 2: Same. I mean, I can tolerate some movement, but I don’t want surprises.

Advisor: Hi Michae, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hey Alex. Honestly, markets have been jumpy and I want a plan that doesn’t blow up our cash flow.
Client 2: Hi. And I’m thinking about retirement timing, like, sooner rather than later, but I don’t want surprises.
Advisor: Totally hear you. Let’s start high level and then we’ll drill down. Michae, what’s the household gross income these days—ballpark for the year?
Client 1: All-in, it’s about two twenty-five a year. Roughly.

Client 2: Hi. And I’m thinking about retirement timing, like, sooner rather than later, but I don’t want surprises.

Client 1: All-in, it’s about two twenty-five a year. Roughly.
Advisor: Okay, about $225k gross household. Margaret, does that sound right to you?

Client 2: Yes, two adults.
Client 1: Just us.
Advisor: Perfect. And you two are married, correct?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Client 1: Yep.

Client 1: Hey Alex. Honestly, markets have been jumpy and I want a plan that doesn’t blow up our cash flow.

Client 2: Yeah, total. I was thinking net of the mortgage at first—sorry.
Advisor: No, that’s super common. So total monthly expenses about $13,000. Then let’s isolate the debt piece inside that. What are the monthly debt payments—mortgage plus any other loan—roughly?
Client 2: Mortgage is… three thousand? Three-one?
Client 1: It’s $3,150 a month on the mortgage.

Advisor: Speaking of taxes—earlier you mentioned withholding on the pension. Do you know what bracket you typically land in? Like 24, 32, 37—ballpark.
Client 2: I thought we were like… 27%? Is that a thing?
Client 1: No—Margaret, that’s not a bracket. We’re in the 37% bracket.
Client 2: Oh—right, sorry. I mix that up with what comes out of the check.
Advisor: Totally fair—people mix up effective rate versus the top bracket. So when you say 37%, Michae, you mean your top federal bracket is 37%, right?

Advisor: Great, thank you. And we’ll still model your actual withholding separately so cash flow matches what hits the bank.
Client 2: Okay, good.
Advisor: One more “context” item, and then I’ll stop with the admin stuff. When you think about the kind of firm you want—are you looking for a US RIA relationship, like fee-based planning and investment management, versus a bank or insurance-only setup?
Client 2: I mean, I thought you were at a bank?
Client 1: No—he’s an RIA. That’s the whole point, Marg.

Client 2: Is that like what actually lands in the checking account each month?
Advisor: Good question—no, that would be net. Gross is the bigger number before taxes. Net is what hits the bank.
Client 1: Gross for me is about one-forty-one thousand a year—like $141,350.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that down. $141,350 gross annually for you, Michae.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to like $150k?

Advisor: $79,400—got it. And I’m seeing you also mentioned something like private markets—Michae, do you have a small allocation there?
Client 1: Yeah, tiny. About $10,150 at the bank.
Advisor: Okay. And then separate from investable assets, your home—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client 2: Around $314,000.
Client 1: $314,100 is what we’ve been using.

Client 1: I’m the primary, yeah. I handle most of it.
Client 2: I mean, I’m involved, but yeah—Michae’s the primary on the money stuff.
Advisor: Got it. And Michae, employment-wise, you’re self-employed, right? Still running your own thing?
Client 1: Yes, self-employed.
Client 2: Well—he’s not “employed” by anyone, so yeah, self-employed.

Advisor: Hi Michae, hi Margaret—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hey Alex. Honestly, markets have been jumpy and I want a plan that doesn’t blow up our cash flow.

Advisor: Michae, quick personal detail while we’re cleaning up the basics—what’s your date of birth?
Client 1: January 8th, 1972.
Advisor: January 8, 1972—thank you. And for your line of work, how would you describe your occupation group? Like professional, skilled trades, that kind of bucket.
Client 1: Professional. I mean—yeah, professional services.
Client 2: He always says “professional,” yes.

Client 2: Yeah.

Client 2: Operations. I’m in operations.
Advisor: Perfect. One more admin-ish thing, and then I’ll drop it—when I label you two in my notes, who’s “Client 1” and who’s “Client 2”?

Client 2: That’s gross, right? Not what hits the checking account.
Advisor: Correct—gross. Net is after taxes and withholdings. Thanks for flagging that.
